1: "LORD, you were favorable to your land; you restored the fortunes of Jacob" — the memory of past restoration (presumably post-exile return) as the ground for present petition. Paul reads Israel's restoration history as prophetic of the cosmic restoration in Christ: "If their rejection means the reconciliation of the world, what will their acceptance mean but life from the dead?" (Romans 11:15). The restoration of Jacob's fortunes becomes the resurrection of the world through the last Jacob — Christ who is Israel-in-person.
2: "You forgave the iniquity of your people; you covered (kissita) all their sin" — the covering of sin as the foundation of restoration. Romans 4:7-8 quotes Psalms 32:1-2: "Blessed is the one whose transgression is forgiven, whose sin is covered." This covering-of-sin is what Christ's atonement accomplishes: "their sins and their lawless deeds I will remember no more" (Hebrews 10:17). The sins covered in Psalms 85:2 are covered by the blood of Christ — the atonement that God was building toward all through Israel's history of forgiveness.
3: "You withdrew all your wrath; you turned from your hot anger" — the withdrawal of divine wrath. Romans 3:25: Christ is the hilasterion (mercy seat / propitiation) "to show God's righteousness, because in his divine forbearance he had passed over former sins." The wrath-withdrawal of Psalms 85:3 was always provisional, anticipating the definitive propitiation in Christ at the cross where God's wrath is fully satisfied and fully withdrawn.
4: "Restore us again, O God of our salvation, and put away your indignation toward us!" — the renewed petition, even after past restoration. The community knows that restoration is not once-for-all in history but ongoing. In Christ, the basis of restored relationship is permanent: Hebrews 10:14: "by a single offering he has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified." The ongoing petition of Psalms 85:4 becomes the ongoing intercession that Christ makes (Hebrews 7:25) — he prays the psalm from within the human experience of needing God's favor.
5: "Will you be angry with us forever? Will you prolong your anger to all generations?" — the anxiety that divine wrath might have no end. Christ answers this anxiety definitively: the Father's permanent favor toward those in the Son means the wrath is not prolonged but absorbed and ended at the cross. "There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus" (Romans 8:1). The anxiety of Psalms 85:5 is the very question that the atonement resolves.
6: "Will you not revive us again, that your people may rejoice in you?" — the prayer for revival, for the giving of new life. Romans 8:11: "If the Spirit of him who raised Jesus from the dead dwells in you, he who raised Christ Jesus from the dead will also give life to your mortal bodies through his Spirit." The revival of Psalms 85:6 is the resurrection-life that flows from Christ's resurrection through the Spirit to his people — the life that makes real rejoicing possible.
7: "Show us your steadfast love, O LORD, and grant us your salvation" — the petition for both hesed and yeshuah (salvation). John 3:16 is the ultimate answer: "For God so loved the world that he gave his only Son, so that whoever believes in him should not perish but have eternal life." The hesed shown and the salvation granted converge in the gift of the Son — the love shown is the salvation given. The double petition of Psalms 85:7 is answered in the single gift of Christ.
8: "Let me hear what God the LORD will speak, for he will speak peace (shalom) to his people, to his faithful ones" — the divine peace-speaking as the oracle of salvation. Christ after the resurrection speaks this peace: "Jesus came and stood among them and said, 'Peace be with you!'" (John 20:19, 21). Ephesians 2:17: "he came and preached peace to you who were far off and peace to those who were near." The divine peace-speaking of Psalms 85:8 is the risen Christ's Easter greeting and the apostolic proclamation of the gospel — the same shalom now embodied and announced by the Prince of Peace.
9: "Surely his salvation is near to those who fear him, so that glory may dwell in our land" — glory dwelling in the land of Israel as the goal of salvation. John 1:14: "the Word became flesh and dwelt (eskenosen, tabernacled) among us, and we have seen his glory." The glory that was to dwell in the land in Psalms 85:9 took up tabernacle-residence in the person of Jesus of Nazareth, in the specific land of Israel. The Incarnation is the fulfillment of the dwelling-glory for which the psalm reaches.
10: "Steadfast love (hesed) and faithfulness (emet) meet; righteousness (tsedeq) and peace (shalom) kiss" — the great convergence of divine attributes at the place of atonement. This verse has been called the theology of the cross in four words. At Calvary: hesed (the covenant love that would not let Israel go) meets emet (the faithful keeping of every promise); tsedeq (the righteous requirement that sin must be punished) kisses shalom (the peace God restores to sinners). Romans 3:26: God is both "just and the one who justifies those who have faith in Jesus." Second Corinthians Psalms 5:21: "God made him who had no sin to be sin for us, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God." The four attributes that seemed irreconcilable are reconciled in the body of Christ on the cross — the kiss of righteousness and peace.
11: "Faithfulness springs up from the earth, and righteousness looks down from heaven" — the cosmic meeting of earth-below and heaven-above. This verse images the Incarnation: emet (faithfulness) "springs up from the earth" — the perfectly faithful human Christ, born of Mary, rooted in Jewish soil, rising from the earth in resurrection; tsedeq (righteousness) "looks down from heaven" — the divine righteousness of the Father, descending in the person of the Son (John 3:13: "No one has ascended into heaven except he who descended"). In the person of Christ, the two directions of Psalms 85:11 converge: heaven's righteousness and earth's faithfulness meet in the Incarnate One.
12: "Yes, the LORD will give what is good, and our land will yield its harvest" — the fecundity of restored creation following divine favor. Romans 8:19-21: "the creation waits with eager longing for the revealing of the sons of God... creation itself will be set free from its bondage to decay." The land yielding its harvest in Psalms 85:12 is the eschatological restoration of all things in Christ — the new creation that flows from the atonement accomplished in vv10-11.
13: "Righteousness will march before him and prepare a path for his steps" — the forerunner of righteousness preparing the way. Isaiah 40:3: "A voice cries: 'In the wilderness prepare the way of the LORD; make straight in the desert a highway for our God.'" Matthew 3:3 identifies this with John the Baptist who prepares the way for Christ: "For this is he who was spoken of by the prophet Isaiah." The righteousness marching before him to prepare his path is John the Baptist as forerunner, and Christ himself as the righteousness that arrives — the forerunner announces what Christ himself is. The final verse of this restoration psalm opens onto the Advent: the one coming in righteousness, prepared for by the way-maker.
